wikitext:
{{Chess Position |Classical variation |parent=[[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...c6|Caro-Kann]] → [[../|2...d5]] |eco=[[Chess/ECOB|B12]] }} == 3. Nc3 · Classical variation == White develops a knight, ready to take back on e4. [[/3...dxe4|'''3...dxe4''']], denying White their two-pawn centre, is the main move, after which White usually recaptures with 4. Nxe4, though some ways of gambitting the pawn may be played. A few sidelines exist if Black wishes to avoid main line theory; * [[/3...Nf6|'''3...Nf6''']] is called the '''Campomanes attack'''. This induces White to play 4. e5, kicking the knight to d7. Black has induced White to play with a closed centre, resembling an [[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...c6/2. d4/2...d5/3. e5|advance variation Caro-Kann]], perhaps not a line they were prepared to play. * [[/3...g6|'''3...g6''']] is the '''Gurgenidze system'''. It allows White to expand on the kingside, 4. e5 Bg7 5. f4 h5, where Black usually looks to trade off their light square bishop and plant their knight on f5 via h5. 4. h3 prevents Black from playing ...Bg4 and trading off the bishop and usually leads to an open centre. * [[/3...b5|'''3...b5?!''']], the '''Gurgenidze counterattack''', where Black hopes to drive off White's knight with ...b4, which they imagine might be good for them. An unsound surprise attack that performs well enough at the club level. '''3...e6?!''', defending the d5 pawn again and combining the disadvantages of both the Caro-Kann and the French defence, must surely be the worse than either. ==Theory table== {{ChessTable}} 1.e4 c6 2.d4 d5 3.Nc3
